lesson: A high hopper reading with product that tastes and pours fine, while an independent probe shows the hopper actually in spec, means a drifted thermistor — not refrigeration failure. Replacing the compressor is the expensive wrong answer.
symptoms: Display says hopper temp is high.

tests.error-log: Recurring HOPPER TEMP HIGH alarm, several times a day for two weeks.
tests.temp-probe: Independent probe reads hopper at 3.2 °C — well in spec. Display still claims 9 °C.
tests.continuity-test: Thermistor resistance is way off the curve for its actual temperature. Sensor has drifted.

symptoms: HOPPER TEMP alarm chirping every few hours.
Staff swear the mix is cold every time they check.

tests.error-log: HOPPER TEMP HIGH logged 41 times this week. Every one self-cleared.
tests.temp-probe: Hand probe puts the hopper at 3.5 °C — in spec. The display insists 8.9 °C.

symptoms: Machine keeps dropping to standby claiming hopper over-temp.
Product texture has been perfect all day.

tests.error-log: Repeated OVER TEMP standby events. No compressor or refrigeration faults logged.
tests.temp-probe: Independent probe reads the hopper at 3.0 °C. The controller is being lied to.

flavour: The machine wasn't warm. It was lying.
authenticityNote: A drifted thermistor reads high while the product is actually fine, so the controller over-runs the compressor. Looks exactly like refrigeration failure — replacing the compressor is the expensive wrong answer (GDD §2.2).
